name: Philip Sarnecki
occupation: Businessman, franchise owner, and faith-based film producer
family: Married to Heidi Sarnecki. Three children raised in Bucyrus. Son of a janitor and a secretary.
whoTheyAre:
Philip Sarnecki is a Kansas businessman from Bucyrus, a small community in the Johnson County area. He grew up the son of a janitor and a secretary, a detail he mentions often on the campaign trail. He and his wife Heidi have raised three children together, and the family also owns a farm in Franklin County.

Sarnecki built his career in financial services, founding RPS Financial Group, which grew to 18 offices across Kansas and served clients in all 50 states. The company managed over $10 billion in assets and served nearly 100,000 Kansas clients. He also acquired several Northwestern Mutual offices, and his firm RPS Benefits by Design became one of the largest life insurance operations in the country. After selling RPS Financial, he became the largest national franchise owner of Strickland Brothers 10 Minute Oil Change, with locations across Kansas in towns like Andover, Derby, Pittsburg, and Fort Scott.

Outside of finance and oil changes, Sarnecki is a co-owner of 513 Films, a faith-based film production company. The company produced "Monster Summer" (2024), which featured Mel Gibson and Kevin James and reached number one on Paramount+. His campaign says the businesses he has built now employ nearly 1,000 people across multiple industries. Sarnecki has never held elected office at any level.
theirRecord:
Philip Sarnecki has no legislative voting record and has never served in public office. His public record is entirely built on private-sector business activity and campaign statements. His campaign presents his business track record as evidence he can run state government effectively, pointing to RPS Financial's growth from a startup to a firm managing billions in assets, plus his expansion of the Strickland Brothers franchise network across Kansas.

At the first Republican gubernatorial debate on January 30, 2026, Sarnecki positioned himself as a political outsider frustrated with career politicians. He attacked the Republican establishment directly, saying politicians have failed to fix property taxes. He also explicitly aligned himself with President Trump and framed his candidacy around "winning races that Republicans have lost in recent cycles." Other debate topics he engaged on included parental rights in education, medical freedom, election integrity, and cutting government regulation.

Because Sarnecki has no government record to evaluate, voters have to weigh his business biography and campaign promises against the legislative records of opponents like Ty Masterson, Scott Schwab, and Jeff Colyer, all of whom have years of documented votes and policy decisions.
whatYouShouldKnow: Sarnecki has never held elected office at any level -- not city council, not school board, not state legislature. This is his first run for any public position.
His campaign is heavily self-financed. Of the $2,528,715.73 raised through the 2025 year-end filing, approximately $2 million came from a personal loan. Outside donations totaled roughly $528,000.
He had $2,306,000 in cash on hand at the end of 2025, more than any other candidate in the entire governor's race from either party.
Sarnecki co-owns 513 Films, a faith-based film production company whose credits include "Monster Summer" (2024) with Mel Gibson and Kevin James. The name "513" appears to reference a Bible verse.
He founded RPS Financial Group, which grew to 18 offices, managed over $10 billion in assets, and served nearly 100,000 Kansas clients before he sold the company.
He is the largest national franchise owner of Strickland Brothers 10 Minute Oil Change, with locations in multiple Kansas communities.
Prediction markets (Polymarket) placed Sarnecki at 13-14% for the Republican nomination as of early 2026, behind frontrunners Jeff Colyer and Ty Masterson but in the competitive middle of the field.
He appeared on Bott Radio Network (Christian radio) and the Real Media KC podcast, framing his campaign around faith, business outsider status, and alignment with President Trump.
whereTheyWorship:
Philip Sarnecki publicly identifies as a Christian, and faith is central to his campaign identity. His campaign advertising closes with a personal appeal to his Christian faith, and he appeared on the Bott Radio Network, a Christian radio outlet, to discuss leadership and the governor's race. His co-ownership of 513 Films, a faith-based film production company, reinforces his connection to the Christian community.

No specific church membership or congregation has been identified in publicly available records. His campaign materials describe him as guided by Christian faith and conservative values, but they do not name a home church or denomination.

church.details: Sarnecki publicly identifies as Christian and centers his faith in campaign messaging, but no specific church or congregation has been identified in public records.

quotes.text: Career politicians are failing us. I am tired of watching Republicans lose in Kansas. No more losing. It's time to start winning again, and that's why I'm running for governor.
I'm just a Kansas dad, husband and businessman.
Philip Sarnecki is a Kansas businessman, family man, and job creator who has built companies that today employ approximately 1,000 people across multiple industries.
Guided by his Christian faith and conservative values, Philip believes in hard work, strong families, and accountable leadership, not career politics.

campaignFinance.narrative: Philip Sarnecki entered the governor's race with more cash on hand than any other candidate in either party. But the story behind that number matters: roughly $2 million of his total came from a personal loan to his own campaign. The remaining roughly $528,000 came from individual donors, a mix of smaller contributions and a few larger checks rather than a broad institutional network. He spent only $222,715.73 through the end of 2025, leaving $2,306,000 in the bank -- suggesting he is stockpiling for a major advertising push closer to the August 2026 primary. The self-funded nature of his campaign means his financial strength comes from personal wealth, not from a large base of grassroots supporters.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: Itemized donor details were not fully parsed from the Kansas Ethics Commission filing. The filing is publicly available as a scanned PDF.
